meta.name: Streets, Sidewalks & Public Places
meta.description: govOS free public service: a plain-language navigator to Maui County Code Title 12 (Streets, Sidewalks & Public Places) — right-of-way work, excavation and driveway permits, encroachment, and who pays. Sourced to Municode + County Public Works; hands off to MAPPS. First version, expanding.
meta.pageTitle: Title 12 — Streets, Sidewalks & Public Places · govOS · Kilo Aupuni
meta.eyebrow: 12 Stones Global · Kilo Aupuni · govOS · public service
meta.h1: Title 12 — Streets, Sidewalks & Public Places Plain-Language Service

hero.lead: A plain-language navigator to Maui County Code Title 12 (Streets, Sidewalks & Public Places): when you need a permit to work in the public right-of-way (excavating a street, cutting a curb, building a driveway, placing anything in a public place), how the process works, and who pays. Administered by Public Works & Waste Management; permits go through MAPPS. Sections marked expanding are being filled in; nothing here is legal advice.

sections.title: ★ Parcel lookup
sections.note: Planning work that touches the street, curb, or sidewalk in front of your property? Enter a Maui County TMK to see the parcel and its frontage context from the Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. The road right-of-way itself is County-owned — most work in it needs a Title 12 permit, applied through MAPPS.

source.html: Live designations: Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. Right-of-way permits via Maui County MAPPS (Public Works). No parcel data is stored or fabricated — fetched live.

sections.title: 1 · Work in the public right-of-way
sections.note: The general path for permitted work in a County street, sidewalk, or public place under Title 12.

steps.name: Get a written permit first
steps.what: No one may break up, dig up, disturb, undermine, or dig under any County highway, street, alley, sidewalk, or other public place without a written permit from Public Works & Waste Management.
steps.pay: Expense: permit fee · paid by: applicant

steps.name: Driveways & curb cuts
steps.what: No one may construct, reconstruct, or repair a driveway, or cut or lower a curb on a County right-of-way, without a written permit from the director (Ch. 12.08).
steps.pay: Expense: permit fee + construction · paid by: applicant

steps.name: Work safely & protect the public
steps.what: The permit holder must use barricades, lights, and watchmen as needed for safety, and may not leave debris, materials, or equipment that creates a hazard.
steps.pay: Expense: safety measures · paid by: permit holder

steps.name: Restore the right-of-way
steps.what: On completion, the permit holder must restore the government right-of-way to good condition; indemnification applies (§12.08.140).
steps.pay: Expense: restoration · paid by: permit holder

position.label: ⚖️ Position — James Langford (analysis, not law; a stance beside the rule)
position.text: The right-of-way is shared public space. Requiring a resident to restore what they disturb is fair. But where the County uses a frontage permit to require a resident to build public improvements that benefit everyone (a new sidewalk, drainage), the cost of the public benefit should be County-borne, not exacted from the one property owner.

source.html: Maui County Code Title 12 — excavation permit requirement + Ch. 12.08 (Driveways), §12.08.140; apply via MAPPS — Permits ↗.

sections.title: 2 · Which Title 12 permit?
sections.note: Common right-of-way activities and where to apply.

rows.v: Excavate / cut a County street or sidewalk
Build / repair a driveway, cut a curb
Place objects / encroach into a public place
Sidewalk in a special improvement area

source.html: Maui County Code Title 12 (§§12.02–12.54); permits via MAPPS / Public Works.

sections.title: 3 · Enforcement
sections.note: Right-of-way violations are reachable through the County enforcement engine. Not legal advice.
sections.html: Title 19's enforcement chapter, §19.530.030, expressly reaches violations of MCC titles 8, 12, 14, 16, 18, 19 and 20 plus related rules and permits — so unpermitted work in the right-of-way (an unauthorized excavation, driveway, or encroachment under Title 12) can draw a notice of violation and order requiring removal/restoration and/or civil fines, final after 30 days unless appealed to the Board of Variances and Appeals.

source.html: Maui County Code Ch. 19.530 (§19.530.030 reach, sourced in the Title 19 service) + Title 12 permit conditions.

sections.title: 4 · Who pays — the charter lens
sections.note: Labeled analysis — the right-of-way read through two charters. Not adopted law; an interpretive lens beside the Code.
sections.mauiCharter: Under the Maui County Charter, Public Works manages and maintains the County's streets, sidewalks, and public places and permits work within them; permit fees and restoration requirements protect the public asset. The Charter frames the ROW as a public infrastructure the department stewards.
sections.sovereignCharter: Under the Sovereign Charter (12 Stones) lens, the street and sidewalk are a commons held in trust for all. Permitting is legitimate to protect that commons; the lens distinguishes restoring what you disturb (the resident's duty) from building new public infrastructure as a permit condition (the County's duty), and resists shifting the latter onto a single owner.

position.label: ⚖️ Position — analysis, not law
position.text: Restore what you break — fair. Fund the public sidewalk the whole neighborhood uses — that's the County's job, not a toll on whoever happens to pull a frontage permit. Same right-of-way, read two ways.

sections.title: 5 · Rules → Code: candidates for codification
sections.intro: Provisions that, in our assessment, Title 12 should make clearer or fairer so residents know their cost and duty. Citations are verbatim-checked; the recommendation is a stance beside the rule.
items: Publish ROW permit fees + restoration standards. → Codify a single readable fee + restoration-spec table so an applicant can price the work up front. expanding
Separate restore-vs-improve. Distinguish restoration (applicant) from new public improvements (County) so frontage permits aren't used to download public infrastructure onto residents.
Encroachment clarity. Codify clear, plain-language rules for what may and may not occupy a public place, so enforcement is predictable.

source.html: Analysis grounded in MCC Title 12; contents expanding pending verbatim ingest.

sections.text: Free now (no account): this page, the live parcel lookup, the ROW navigator, and the permit table are all Tier 1 — free. Coming — Tier 3 (verified tools): a right-of-way permit-readiness checklist and an AI Title-12 assistant will be verification-gated (identity-verified). Identity is verified through the payment processor — only verified-status is stored here, never the raw ID. No live billing is enabled (Stripe keys are environment references only); the actual permit application happens on MAPPS.
